title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

texts.A: The shift toward permanent remote work is often hailed as a victory for employee autonomy, but this overlooks the profound loss of 'social capital.' [37]While some managers fear a drop in productivity, the real danger is the erosion of the spontaneous, informal interactions—the 'water cooler' moments—that drive innovation.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the blurring of boundaries between home and office is leading to a silent epidemic of burnout, as workers find themselves 'always on' and unable to disconnect.[/39] Cohesive team culture requires shared physical space where relationships develop organically, well beyond the confines of scheduled video calls and carefully edited written communications. Junior employees in particular suffer disproportionately in remote environments, deprived of the mentorship, observational learning, and informal feedback loops that are essential for early career development. Organizations that normalize permanent remote work without addressing these structural inequalities will find themselves with a fragmented workforce whose collective capability steadily erodes over time.
texts.B: The idea that work must happen in a specific building between 9 and 5 is a relic of the industrial age. Digital tools have proven that most knowledge work can be done from anywhere, with no loss in output. [38]I disagree with those who claim that remote work kills culture; a strong company culture is built on shared values and clear communication, not on shared breathing space.[/38] [40]Moreover, the environmental benefits of removing millions of cars from the daily commute are too significant to ignore.[/40] [37]I share the view that we are witnessing a long-overdue rebalancing of the power dynamic between employer and employee.[/37] For the first time in the history of employment, workers can live where they choose and participate in labor markets without uprooting their families. This geographic freedom has already revitalized smaller cities and rural communities drained by decades of urbanization. Employers who insist on mandatory in-office attendance without compelling justification will increasingly find themselves unable to compete for the most talented workers in the market.
texts.C: The move to remote work is a double-edged sword. On one hand, it allows for a more diverse workforce, including those with caregiving responsibilities or disabilities. [40]On the other hand, the claim that it is 'greener' is questionable, as the energy saved on commuting is often offset by the increased heating and cooling requirements of individual homes.[/40] [38]I share the view that remote work can lead to a 'stagnation of ideas' because video calls are far less effective than face-to-face meetings for creative brainstorming.[/38] A well-designed hybrid policy—one that defines which tasks are best performed collaboratively in person and which are better suited to individual focused work at home—can capture the benefits of both models without the drawbacks of either extreme. Organizations that impose a one-size-fits-all approach, whether demanding full office attendance or permitting fully remote work indefinitely, will inevitably underserve some segment of their workforce. The solution is not a total return to the office, but a thoughtful hybrid model.
texts.D: The real beneficiaries of the remote work revolution are not the employees, but the corporations that can now outsource jobs to lower-wage regions. [39]I share the concern that without the physical separation of the workplace, the 'right to disconnect' is becoming a luxury of the past.[/39] [37]However, I am less convinced than some that remote work will lead to a decline in innovation; many of the world's most successful open-source projects were built by people who never met in person.[/40] [37]The problem isn't where we work, but how we manage our time in a digital world.[/37] The office was often a theater of 'performative busyness' rather than a true hub of creativity. The fundamental questions about remote work are ultimately questions about power and trust: do employers trust their employees to work effectively without surveillance, and do workers trust their employers to respect the boundaries that make sustainable performance possible? Building genuine mutual accountability requires radical honesty about expectations and outcomes, not passive monitoring tools deployed in the name of productivity.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to A regarding the impact of remote work on innovation and creativity?
questions.explanation: A believes the loss of informal interactions will 'drive innovation' down. D disagrees, stating they are 'less convinced' it leads to a decline, citing open-source projects as proof of remote success.

questions.question: Which expert shares C's view that the quality of collaboration suffers in a remote-only environment?
questions.explanation: C believes remote work leads to a 'stagnation of ideas' because video calls are less effective than face-to-face meetings. A shares this view, mourning the loss of 'spontaneous, informal interactions' that drive innovation.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the effect of remote work on the mental health and boundaries of employees?
questions.explanation: A mentions a 'silent epidemic of burnout' and the inability to disconnect. D also shares this concern, stating the 'right to disconnect' is becoming a luxury of the past due to lack of physical separation.

questions.question: Which expert has a different opinion to B on the environmental impact of working from home?
questions.explanation: B argues that the environmental benefits (fewer cars) are 'too significant to ignore.' C, however, argues this is 'questionable' because the energy saved on commuting is offset by increased domestic energy use.
